sourceMovie.title: Smile
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Smile works as a horror movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The horror and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of Smile is alive in The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It (2021), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Final Destination 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Final Destination 2 (2003) operates in the same horror as Smile, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Malignant
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Malignant (2021) belongs to the same conversation as Smile — both anchor their horror in mystery emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Mirrors
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Mirrors (2008) comes earlier and helps explain the emotional blueprint that Smile later built on. Watch it as a predecessor to see where those themes and character tensions started.

recommendations.title: Stonehearst Asylum
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Smile excels in horror, Stonehearst Asylum (2014) doubles down on mystery to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Come Play
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Come Play (2020) is the natural next step after Smile: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and mystery elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Psycho II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Smile who respond to its horror will find Psycho II (1983) immediately familiar. Both films share a mystery sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: The Entity
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Smile fans keep landing on The Entity (1982) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Sherlock Holmes
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Sherlock Holmes draws from the same mystery well as Smile, channeling adventure in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2009 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Halloween
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Halloween (2018) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that Smile later expanded. If Smile resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

faq.question: What should I watch after Smile?
faq.answer: Try The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It, Final Destination 2, Malignant—each shares DNA with Smile while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Smile?
faq.answer: Smile (2022) is best described as Horror, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Smile part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Smile Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
